title: Oxygen Therapy: Safe Delivery and Device Selection
subtitle: A patient on the medical unit becomes short of breath with a falling SpO2. The nurse selects the correct oxygen device, titrates to the target saturation, and applies oxygen-safety precautions while watching for hypoxia.

patient.name: Oxygen Therapy
patient.admitDate: A hospitalized patient becomes dyspneic with a falling oxygen saturation. The nurse chooses the right delivery device, titrates to target SpO2, and enforces oxygen-safety precautions.

labs.v: Add for higher flow / long use.

nursesNote: A patient on the medical unit becomes increasingly short of breath with a falling SpO2. Oxygen is a drug: the nurse gives the lowest effective dose to correct hypoxemia and titrates to the ordered target saturation. Device choice matches the need — a nasal cannula (1-6 L/min) for low-flow needs, a simple face mask (run at least 5 L/min to flush CO2), a Venturi mask for a precise FiO2, and a non-rebreather (10-15 L/min, reservoir bag kept inflated) for the highest concentration in emergencies. The nurse adds humidification for higher flows, checks the nares/ears for skin breakdown, and monitors pulse oximetry and work of breathing. In a COPD CO2 retainer the target is ~88-92% — oxygen is never withheld from a hypoxic patient. Oxygen supports combustion, so the nurse keeps flames, sparks, smoking, and petroleum products away and posts an 'Oxygen in Use' sign. Restlessness, tachypnea, and tachycardia are early hypoxia; cyanosis and bradycardia are late.
providerOrders: Apply oxygen to correct hypoxemia; titrate to the ordered target SpO2
Match the device to the need (cannula, simple/Venturi mask, or non-rebreather)
Run a simple mask at >=5 L/min and keep the non-rebreather bag inflated
Add humidification for higher flows; monitor SpO2 and skin integrity
Enforce oxygen fire safety; never withhold oxygen from a hypoxic COPD patient

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about oxygen delivery and hypoxia?

opts.text: A nasal cannula at 1-6 L/min delivers low-flow oxygen and lets the patient eat and talk
A simple face mask should run at least 5 L/min to flush exhaled CO2
A non-rebreather mask delivers the highest oxygen concentration of these devices
Oxygen is flammable and will catch fire on its own with no ignition source
Early hypoxia often shows as restlessness, tachypnea, and tachycardia
Cyanosis is an early, reliable sign of hypoxia
A Venturi mask delivers a precise, fixed FiO2 useful in COPD
The reservoir bag on a non-rebreather must stay inflated during breathing
Humidification is added for higher flow rates or longer use
In a COPD CO2 retainer, target an SpO2 of about 88-92% rather than 100%
Oxygen should be withheld from a hypoxic COPD patient to protect their drive
A petroleum-based lip balm is safe to use around running oxygen

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the response to sudden dyspnea with SpO2 85% on room air

opts.text: Raise the head of the bed to an upright position
Apply oxygen with the appropriate device and titrate to target
Reassess SpO2, work of breathing, and lung sounds
Stay with the patient and notify the provider
Document the event, intervention, and response
Posting an 'Oxygen in Use' sign and keeping open flames away
Allowing a visitor to smoke near the oxygen source
Running a simple face mask at 2 L/min
Keeping the non-rebreather reservoir bag inflated
Titrating a COPD patient to an SpO2 of 88-92%
Using a petroleum-based lubricant on dry nares near oxygen
Adding humidification for higher-flow oxygen
Setting the flow above the device's recommended range

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence applying a non-rebreather to a hypoxic patient
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess the patient and confirm the need and target SpO2
Connect the mask and set flow to fully inflate the reservoir bag
Apply the mask with a snug, comfortable fit
Reassess SpO2, respiratory effort, and comfort
Document and notify the provider of the response
Titrating oxygen to the lowest level that maintains the target SpO2
Leaving a hypoxic patient to gather supplies before giving oxygen
Keeping flames, sparks, and smoking away from oxygen
Running a simple face mask below 5 L/min
Checking nares and ears for skin breakdown from the cannula
Withholding oxygen from a hypoxic COPD patient
Adding humidification and monitoring pulse oximetry
Letting the non-rebreather reservoir bag deflate during breathing
